content_english: I also run a ride-hailing service with my VF6. I once broke into a cold sweat because I ran the car down to 0% battery. The situation is quite similar to what your friend shared. From Nhon Trach to Ho Chi Minh City. 4km before getting on the Cat Lai ferry, the battery dropped to 0%, and while on the ferry, I wondered if turning off the car at 0% would recover a bit of battery or if it wouldn't start again, so I decided to keep the car running (not turning it off) until the intersection of Dong Van Cong - Nguyen Thi Dinh. At that point, the battery was at 0%, but the car only allowed a speed of 16km/h, and the air conditioning turned off automatically. I continued moving at a snail's pace (16km/h) to the charging station another 2.7km away. Yet the car made it without stalling, battery still at 0% when I reached the charging station, and it charged normally. A risky and hair-raising experience. The VF6's battery is very okay (I estimate that at 0% battery, the car can still travel another 8km based on my feeling).

content_english:
I noticed in the video: the storage space in the car is also large, no windshield HUD, no blind spot warning detected (when a car is driving alongside), range is similar to the VF8 Plus.

If charging at home — without a private garage — it also incurs high residential electricity rates, so the electricity cost won't be as favorable as the dealer says.

In short, the car owner must have a huge amount of trust in the promises of BYD showrooms to buy this car for ride-hailing service; other brands should look at which guy can step up and successfully sell a car for this kind of service, then hire him — this guy is definitely a marketing talent.

category: Value Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; Other
parameter: Price; Resale Value; Battery Replacement Cost; Service Use Suitability
content_english: As a ride-hailing driver who's already used two cars, skip electric cars and you can buy a used Stargazer standard version for around 450 million, 300 million cheaper. It runs on gasoline, and after 250,000 km or more, if you want to change cars, if you bought a used Stargazer, selling it also holds its value much better than an electric car. When selling, you also don't have to worry about replacing the battery to be able to resell it. For ride-hailing, the car doesn't stay with you for long.

content_english: That's a good feature, but being able to drive further even after hitting 0% is actually normal. How much extra you can go depends on how the manufacturer sets up the BMS and power chain. The root cause is that the battery's charge/discharge curve is non-linear, with the most variation near full charge and near empty. So manufacturers calculate battery percentage within a range that can be roughly considered linear (where capacity drops relatively evenly), meaning 100% is set at the point where the battery begins to discharge steadily, and 0% is where it stops being steady. Additionally, manufacturers may set 0% at an even earlier point as a reserve for unexpected situations, like the driver in the video experienced (7:15). In short, 0% on the display doesn't necessarily mean the battery is completely drained, and 100% doesn't necessarily mean it's fully charged.

content_english: China can produce good batteries, but saying that cars made in China use the best batteries isn't certain, because it also depends on how much the car manufacturer spends to buy the batteries for the car. content_english: I just test drove the M6. The car feels very well put together, with soft, smooth leather. The electric motor goes without saying. Sound insulation is quite good, and the suspension is comfortable. Two major issues: first, the car has noticeable lateral swaying—if you get carsick easily, you won’t like that. Second, at that price point, it should come with ADAS. Oh, and one more thing: the speaker system is a bit underwhelming. I currently drive a pre-2023 Xp, and its suspension doesn’t have that swaying issue.

As for the VF6 to VF9, they're probably mostly sold to business partners, since you rarely see private plates on the road! Prices from the VF6 upward are also outrageous in their segment—electric is cheaper than gasoline, but it's practical for ride-hailing use; for someone commuting 1,500 km a month, it's very hard to convince customers to accept battery swaps and the risk of operational issues. I hope VinFast sets the right price, then electric cars can truly develop!

Moreover, in China, they push car prices as low as possible to capture the market first, instead of setting sky-high prices like Vin and then gradually reducing them. Using a gasoline car feels like burning money, and buyers get more and more fed up. They'd rather buy a used car than ever spend their money on a new Vin. Also, recently Vin has been trending toward not releasing improved models, but just reusing old design versions, adding a little, and selling at a high price. Personally, I'd rather bear the higher maintenance cost of a gasoline car than buy a Vin electric car at this time.

content_english: In principle, investing in a car to run as a taxi still brings in business, but of course it will be more limited. Instead, if you drive a Vinfast, you can comfortably take long trips without restrictions on passengers. As for your car, it's only suitable for passengers going short distances or those who can wait to charge (even when arriving at the destination, you still have to charge for 7-8 hours like others). Therefore, the revenue and income may not necessarily be higher than those driving a Vinfast or a gasoline car. Not to mention, if there are more cars, there will be a shortage of charging slots; passengers will experience it a few times and then won't dare to book again. Currently, Vinfast hasn't released an MPV yet, so some people with demand are considering choosing BYD to compete with gasoline cars. In a few months, if Vinfast launches an MPV, then fewer people will choose BYD, because they will weigh between price and convenience.

People who buy cars for ride-hailing services are very concerned about maintenance costs and resale value. The cost of replacing batteries and spare parts will be quite expensive and scarce. Not to mention that reselling the car is also difficult, and if you can sell it, it will likely lose value! Therefore, the majority of buyers of this car are wealthy people who want to experience it, while ride-hailing service customers will find it hard to choose this car!

category: Value Perception; Brand Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; New Tech; Brand Perception
parameter: Maintenance Cost; After Sales Service; Battery Replacement Cost; Resale Value; Charging Infrastructure; Brand Trust
content_english:
To be honest, this M6 is not viable for ride-hailing service! For service vehicles, the priority is cost efficiency. Besides maintenance costs, you also have to consider after-sales support, service centers, spare parts, and replacement components. BYD’s battery pack costs no less than 200 million VND. And then there's resale value—selling the car later! For an old electric car without proper charging infrastructure, who would dare buy it secondhand?  

Also, what about customer demand? Do passengers actually want to ride in that car? Would they trust using an electric vehicle without a reliable network of charging stations for long trips?

content_english:
To be honest, for a 7-seater ride-hailing vehicle, the Vinfast Limo Green is still the best! Just the fact that it can operate on both Grab and Xanh SM already puts it ahead of the competition. The battery replacement cost for the Limo Green is only around 90-120 million VND, half the price of a BYD battery, while durability is comparable since both use LFP chemistry.

Moreover, Vinfast has a massive dataset on driving habits of ride-hailing drivers, along with durability and parts quality, so they can fine-tune the car to suit heavy-duty work. As for anyone claiming BYD is better for ride-hailing, prove it!

category: Value Perception; Other; Other; Brand Perception
parameter: Maintenance Cost; Vehicle Lifespan; Crash Repairability; Brand Image
content_english: A new game begins. Before, Vin covered all charging stations, but now Vin won't let me charge, I won't sell the battery. Later, with a monopoly, Vin raised battery prices and spare part prices even more. In the end, the Vietnamese people bear it all. Electric cars run 6, 7, 8 years, replacing the battery costs 70% of the car's value. Gasoline cars run 40, 50 years, even 100 years, running 2 or 3 million kilometers only need a cylinder replacement. When an electric car crashes, the motor can't be repaired, it's thrown away. A gasoline car's engine can still be fixed and run. In short, electric cars are for the wealthy; after 10 years, buy another one.

category: New Tech; Brand Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Technology Level; Build Quality; After Sales Service
content_english: In my personal opinion, currently Chinese cars are top in the world in terms of technology, and their quality is also very good. However, buying a car that truly earns customer trust and loyalty involves many other factors, such as the overall experience, after-sales service, commitments, and reliable customer support — areas where China hasn't done well yet. After all, as the world's factory, if you produce a wide variety of things, you can't specialize in any one of them. Now competition requires many factors; if a car brand listens to users and provides attentive care, that brand will definitely win widespread support.

parameter: Brand Trust; Air Conditioning; Value for Money; Acceleration
content_english: The first car I bought was a VF8, so I spent a long time driving it and dealing with various issues and repairs, but I still support electric cars, really. And I have a high regard for BYD—I've ridden many BYD cars used for ride-hailing services, and I have to admit it's true: in Thailand, even with such hot weather, I sat in a BYD and had to cover myself with a blanket because the AC was so cold I wasn't freezing to death. As for the VF8, I had to get its air conditioner fixed, it was way too extreme. I bought it in the first batch, so it had quite a few issues, and I sold it—I don't know if the new owner has found any faults yet. As for BYD, the brand is so strong; when I got in the car, I was overwhelmed and thought it was a luxury car being used for ride-hailing. This is my honest experience with BYD—the main thing is that in Vietnam, charging isn't convenient enough yet; with the VF, I charge very easily.

content_english: Those "gurus" have been praising since 2023, but the car has only sold a few units. They're all keyboard warriors who won't buy anything—how could a BYD car ever sell in Vietnam? First, if you have 1.2 billion, you buy a VF8, not a BYD Seal, right? 900 million? Buy a VF7, not an Atto3, right? Soon Limogreen will release something, and it will immediately call out the M6. If you buy a car for practical use, it's VF; if you buy a car to show off, then what, BYD? Only a handful buy BYD—without charging stations, it's no different from a gas car with no gas station. A customer going to Thường Xuân (Thanh Hóa) has to detour back to Thanh Hóa city to charge? In general, this "guru" is fake and only talks about ideal conditions. That guy Giang bought the car and then went all over forums praising it as if it's right. I've driven all kinds of cars, and this one still has plenty of flaws, kaka.

content_english: Don't trust the warranty policy too easily; who’s to say you haven’t violated the warranty terms, like the requirement not to let the battery drop below 5% or 10%. Some days you go far and work isn’t easy, by the time you’re at 30% looking for a charging station and there’s none nearby, it’s very easy to end up with a low battery. So do your research before believing only half of what those policies say. The VF e34 used for ride-hailing has done 160,000 km in less than 2 years and the battery is still at 86% — no idea when it’ll drop below 70%, and if it does, will they even replace it or not.

The issue here is where to charge... because most people in Vietnam live in apartments without space to build safe charging stations and three-phase power needed for proper charging of EVs. Honestly, if BYD invests properly in the Vietnamese market and builds charging stations like Vin does, then our VinFast won't be able to compete fairly with this company. But it's difficult for BYD to invest in charging stations like Vin, because the Vietnamese car market is very small compared to other key markets. That's the real issue.

parameter: Charging Speed; Charging Infrastructure; Ride-hailing Suitability
content_english: This review probably comes from someone who has owned an electric car as their main vehicle, which is why they have such a review. Over a year ago, I bought an e34 and sold my Ertiga because my house had a 10m frontage, so I set up home charging right away (the reviewer mentioned buying an 11kW charger without knowing that residential power can only support a maximum of 7.5kW direct connection, and the wiring from the meter to the house; anything above 7.5kW requires three-phase power, which very few homes have for residential use). Two conditions to install a 7.5kW charger: you need a parking spot inside the house plus a direct connection right at the meter. Meeting both conditions in central Saigon or Hanoi? Probably no one would buy it. I ran into trouble when I moved to District 7—I couldn't park the car inside because the road was wide, but the curb was deliberately built 3 steps high, so no car could enter the house to charge. That's when I thought about using public charging stations, but there aren't many in District 7, making it very inconvenient when I needed to charge. In the end, I sold the car at a loss. As for BYD, there's only one YouTuber who told the truth: when driving to Can Tho, a third-party charger only delivers 5kW, and with this battery, it takes over 10 hours to fully charge—note that the largest city in the Mekong Delta only has a few charging stations. And with VinFast's charging network, even in District 7, Saigon, a year ago, I saw very few spots, let alone BYD's third-party chargers, which are slow (5-7kW is considered slow charging). For an ordinary person like me, I can't even tolerate that, let alone buying one for ride-hailing. Ride-hailing drivers rely on their time for profit, but with such long charging times, how can they make it work? This car is suited for suburban drivers with spacious homes, where they can park inside, install a 7.5kW home charger, charge overnight, and drive in the morning—but they can't drive intercity because the risk of running out of battery is very high; there are no charging stations in rural areas, so even though the battery gauge shows how much is left, you can end up stuck with no nearby charger. That's just a dead end. All EV owners dread the charging process; only those who've never owned an electric car don't understand. So anyone wanting to buy one for ride-hailing should think carefully before purchasing, or else you'll end up stuck—can't keep it, can't sell it either. It's a huge loss. I sold my e34 to a guy who bought it for ride-hailing, and from talking to him, I know well that buying on installment to pay off debt is a huge pressure, supporting a family and paying off the car loan. After over a year, I think he's probably given up, but with this m6, I'm not sure.

content_english: Operation is good, aesthetics are beautiful. When you open the door, guys completely forget it's a Chinese-made car. Buying a used one is a different story. It lacks low-end torque, and the distance traveled is still short. Especially for someone like me who buys a car for a cross-Vietnam trip, buying a used car like I do, only about 3 out of 10 people do that. For the rest of you guys using it daily for commuting, it's amazing. Because the story of using an electric car versus a gasoline car: the level of savings with an electric car is night and day. In my opinion, those cars from your country producing PHEVs are more on point. According to the manufacturer, they announced only 1L/100km. To achieve that cost saving, even raising it to 3L/100km is still an incredible number when you guys open the door and step into the car—it's so happy. When you guys think about a pure gasoline car now with the same functionality as a BYD M6, you have to pay 8-9L, sometimes over 10L/100km.

content_english: To be honest, I don't like electric cars. I also have a gasoline car. But recently, I wanted to get a BYD M6 to try out. My hometown is Đồng Tháp. To be honest, BYD is really well made. And the dealership even lends you a portable charger for home charging, so even when I'm back in my hometown, I'm not worried about the battery. Although the charging stations aren't as many as VinFast's, whenever I go somewhere, there are franchise charging stations that can charge BYD anyway.

parameter: Charging Station Availability; Sales Volume; Charging Infrastructure Cost; Charging Station Availability
content_english: Relying on private charging stations to develop is extremely difficult. Because for a high-power station (60kW and up), the minimum investment cost is 500 million. Don't be fooled by Vin's advertised price of 250 million for a 60kW station as the final price—the actual construction cost is the real kicker. You driving the M6 in the article can stick to a fixed route like that, but the key factor is that "BYD SELLS TOO FEW CARS." That's why you can always find a spot to charge when you arrive at a station. If there were many charging stations, when you can't charge at one, you could go to another. But if there's only one station in the area and it's completely full, then your travel plans are ruined—especially if you're on a passenger pick-up route.

content_english: People praising BYD from 2023 till now, but the car has only sold a few units? All keyboard warriors—who would actually buy a BYD car in Vietnam? First, if you have 1.2 billion VND, you'd buy a VF8, not a BYD Seal. For 900 million, you'd buy a VF7, not an Atto3. Soon, when Limogreen launches and starts deliveries, the Alo M6 will be right there. If you buy a car for practical daily use, go with VF; if you buy a car for show and luxury, why would you pick BYD? Only a few people buy BYD—what if you run out of charge and there's no difference from a gas car running out of gas? A customer from Thuong Xuan (Thanh Hoa) would have to loop back to Thanh Hoa city just to charge? In short, these "experts" are clueless and only talk based on ideal conditions. That guy Giang is a guy who bought one after being misled and then goes on every forum praising it like crazy. I've driven all kinds of cars, and this one still has plenty of flaws, lol.

category: Value Perception; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception
content_english: Don't trust the warranty policy just like that. Who knows if you've even violated the warranty terms—for example, the requirement not to let the battery drop below 5% or 10%, but some days you have to go far and work, it's not easy to find a charger when you're down to 30%, so the battery gets very low. So research carefully before blindly believing one-sided statements from those policies. The VF e34 has run 160,000 km in less than 2 years, and its battery health is still 86%. No idea when it'll drop below 70%, and then will they replace it or not?

content_english: Vin pushes strong marketing to boost sales, so they also have to push the number of charging stations. But the downside is that Vin's product experience is low, only about a third of BYD's while the price is similar, and on top of that Vin offers more promotions :))) But honestly, in terms of vehicle refinement, Vin is far inferior to BYD, not even worth comparing. One company focuses on marketing, boosting sales, and being dominant, while the other focuses on product quality but has weak infrastructure. It just depends on your usage needs when buying a car—for example, if you drive for a ride-hailing service, Vin is actually better; but if you're a regular user aiming for a good experience, BYD completely outclasses Vin.

content_english: In my opinion, VinFast hasn't done it because if they did, the price wouldn't be competitive with existing 7-seater vehicles used for ride-hailing services. In reality, even the main models VF5 and VF3 haven't been able to achieve 80,000 units in sales this year. As for the VF6-VF9, it seems most are sold to partners, with few white-plate private cars seen on the road. Prices for the VF6 and above are also dominant in their segment—electric cars are cheaper than gas ones, but for ride-hailing drivers practicality matters, while for daily commuters driving 1,500 km per month, it's very hard to convince customers to accept battery replacement and the risk of operational issues. I hope VinFast sets the right real price, so electric vehicles can truly develop!

content_english: The video says the battery is good, no range reduction, about 400km, but at the end of the video it's 350km? Not really complaining but feels a bit off. I often travel Hanoi-Hai Duong, once talked to a friend driving the M6 on that route—no issues with the car, but the charging problem is the worst, because charging stations are rare, sometimes you have to wait over an hour just for your turn. The seating in this car is much more comfortable than the Xp and Veloz, not as cramped as the VF6. Compared to the Limo, the seating is about the same, but the Limo feels smoother.

parameter: Battery Range; Technology; Build Quality; Brand Nationalism
content_english: Many Vietnamese brothers have tested the Limo Green car, which is advertised to run about 450km, but they actually ran more, even over 500km. BYD claims to run 420km, and a driver said that after about 80,000km it still runs close to that figure, but can it actually exceed it? VinFast loses in technology, but makes up for it with much better mechanisms. Simply put, the VF5 is advertised to run 320km, but in reality it runs much more—some people have gotten up to 400km. So isn't the comparison above uneven? If Vietnamese people don't support Vietnamese products, then who will? Now why suddenly make a video like this? Why not directly highlight the strength where VinFast actually excels? Limo is cheaper and gets free charging—so given the price difference compared to BYD, isn't that the smarter choice?

The Limo is cheaper and comes with free charging, so factoring that in against the small price gap with the BYD, isn't that a no-brainer?

category: Value Perception; Other; Safety & ADAS; Value Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Battery Replacement Cost; Charging Time; Vehicle Reliability; Maintenance Cost; Country of Origin Image
content_english:
The electric car looks great, the Vin car also looks great. But sorry, I still choose Japanese cars.

*7-seat electric car. On-road price including spare tire, battery protection, leather seat covers... around 750 million. Running 4 years = 320,000 km - 10,000 km for charging detours = remaining 310,000 km running as shared taxi × 6,000 VND/km fare = 1,860 million VND revenue.
- Subtract 128 million for electricity (160,000 km × 800 VND/km over 2 years after free charging ends)
- Subtract 720 million for driver salary (48 months × 15 million)
- Subtract 312 million for loan interest (48 months × 6.5 million)
- Subtract 48 million for tires (16 tires × 3 million)
- Subtract 44 million for insurance (vehicle, battery, personal)
- Subtract 70 million for maintenance and repairs + battery removal, motor disassembly, coolant replacement for battery, motor bearing replacement...
- Subtract 28 million for road tolls and car washes
- Subtract 150 million for replacing worn-out battery
Total expenses = 1,500 million.
Total revenue 1,860 mil - 1,500 mil = 360 mil profit - 360 mil depreciation = zero.

*7-seat gasoline car. On-road price 600 million.
Running 4 years, 310,000 km × 6,000 fare = 1,860 million.
- Subtract 434 million for gasoline (310,000 km × 1,400 VND/km)
- Subtract 720 million for salary (48 months × 15 million)
- Subtract 259 million for loan interest (48 months × 5.4 million)
- Subtract 24 million for tires (12 tires × 2 million)
- Subtract 25 million for vehicle and personal insurance
- Subtract 70 million for maintenance + repairs + oil change + air filter, fuel filter
- Subtract 28 million for road tolls and car washes
Total expenses 1,560 million.
Revenue 1,860 mil - expenses 1,560 mil = 300 mil profit - 300 mil depreciation = zero.

Disadvantages of electric cars: battery, electricity, and spare parts may increase in price because currently they are priced low to stimulate sales volume. The cars often pose safety risks and affect work time. Risk of expensive parts failing costing from tens to hundreds of millions like DBU, power cables, inverter, battery casing, battery cell, and the AC filter also needs to cool the battery so it's both expensive and wears out faster. Inconvenient when you have to spend 1-2 hours charging. While a gasoline car rarely breaks down. If you use proper oil, the engine can run up to 500,000 km and only costs 50 million for an overhaul, which is good profit.

Currently BYD quotes a 60kWh battery at around 250 million. While VinFast's battery price is valued at 1/3 of the original battery cost, which seems unreasonable. Is Mr. Vuong pricing the battery low to lure people (like a scam)? Because currently the batteries are still new, so pricing them low has no effect. A few years from now when the batteries fail, if they raise the price to market level, nobody will dare complain.

parameter: Brand Trust; Incentives; Cost Savings; Warranty; Earning Potential; Reliability; Exterior Design; Ride Quality; General Quality; General Technology; Weight
content_english:
Caring about quality means caring based on trust. If you don’t trust, don’t buy—no need for fake politeness either =))

Frankly, people buy VF cars because they trust the quality (I don’t know who has better quality, but trusting is enough; time will tell). They get price discounts, charging benefits, better battery support, and they’re also willing to support a car brand that manufactures in Vietnam. You guys criticize, but those old cars only sell a few hundred units per year in Vietnam—nobody knows them. The people who praise them actually buy them, selling several thousand units every month.

Instead of sweating to earn a hundred bucks, instead of spending 200k/day (6 million/month) on gas, they’re relaxed and save 6 million. OK? And 6 million is nothing—I’ve even met guys who charge twice a day, spending 8-9 million a month on gas. After one year, they save an extra 70-100 million.

I drive a VF5, plug it into GSM to run extra hours on the side, drive nearly 2,000 km a month, earn 7-8 million, and get free charging for 3.5 years until the end of 2027 (I have an extra 0.5 year early adopter bonus for buying the battery). The car is crappy, ugly, stiff, heavy, and outdated tech-wise, but it hasn’t spent a single day in the shop.

parameter: Energy Consumption; Value for Money; Maintenance Cost
content_english: It's a fuel-efficient model, but throughout its life, it has to carry a 300kg gasoline engine on board—so is it really economical? It's a simple math problem. Even if its battery has higher amp hours, it doesn't necessarily mean more kilometers. Because it has to haul that gasoline engine around. So that's a loss of efficiency, meaning extra money spent on charging, not to mention installation and other costs.

parameter: Battery Range; Tech Comparison; Competitor Comparison
content_english: Many Vietnamese guys have tested the Limo Green and according to the official claim of about 450km, they actually got more, even over 500km. BYD officially claims 420km, and a driver said after about 80,000 km on the odometer, the range is still close to that figure, but can it exceed? Vinfast loses in technology, but more than makes up for it in chassis – simply put, the VF5 claims 320km, but in reality it gets much more, some people have gotten up to 400km. So isn't the comparison above skewed? If Vietnamese don't support Vietnamese products, who will? Now why make a video like this out of nowhere? Why not just focus on the area where Vinfast does better? The Limo is cheaper and gets free charging, so considering the small price difference (compared to BYD), is it really inferior?

The remaining one should be a stretched VF3 or VFe34 to add a third row, using the VF6 engine.

parameter: Running Cost; Charging Network; Battery Quality; Country of Origin Image
content_english: Calculating fuel costs, it's only about half that of a hybrid. Too bad the charging stations are still too few, so it's not suitable for all regions. category: Value Perception; Value Perception; Brand Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Battery Cost; Competitor Comparison; Service Compatibility; Data Advantage
content_english: To be honest, for running a 7-seat ride-hailing service, the Vinfast Limo Green is still the best! Just the fact that it can operate on both Grab and Xanh SM already puts it ahead of other competitors. The battery replacement cost for the Limo Green is only around 90 to 120 million VND, half the price of a BYD battery, while durability is comparable since both use the same LFP material. Moreover, Vinfast has a massive data source on the driving habits of ride-hailing drivers, plus durability and parts quality, so they will fine-tune the car to suit the grind. As for anyone claiming BYD is better for ride-hailing, prove it!

parameter: Competitor Comparison; Sales Performance; Price; Ownership Experience
content_english: In my opinion, VF hasn't done it yet because if they did, the price couldn't compete with the existing 7-seaters used for ride-hailing services. Actually, even the VF5 and VF3 haven't been able to push sales to 80k units this year. The VF6 to VF9 are probably mostly sold to partners, as you rarely see them with private plates on the road! Prices from the VF6 upward are also dominating their segment — electric is cheaper than gas, and it's practical for service vehicles, but for daily commuters driving 1,500 km per month, it's very hard to convince customers to accept battery replacement and the risk of operational issues! Hopefully VF will set the right price; only then can electric vehicles truly take off!

category: Powertrain / Performance; Other; Other; Brand Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Battery Range; Charging Infrastructure; General Impression; Brand Image; After Sales Service
content_english: This car should only be driven within a 180km radius from home to still have enough charge to return — that's reasonable. If you try to use it for long-distance passenger rides, forget it; good luck finding a charging station, they won't listen. This car has no flaws to speak of, but anyone buying it for ride-hailing services, especially first-time car buyers choosing an EV, should think carefully. Right now, the number of VF vehicles sold is overwhelming. If VF doesn't upgrade its charging station network and keep its service centers and showrooms on top of maintenance, then in a year or two, VF will be in trouble.

content_english: BYD has too few charging stations, which puts it at a disadvantage compared to VinFast. On top of that, it's a Chinese brand—even in China, no matter how good they are, from a Vietnamese perspective, we really don't like Chinese brands, especially since they just entered Vietnam. So even though the M6 is priced at 756 million, many guys still choose a 2022-2023 Xpander for ride-hailing, since maintenance is cheap and the used car is also affordable. As for the 7-seat family segment, people would probably go with the Innova for peace of mind, rather than a Chinese brand with few charging stations. But if the price dropped to around 700 million, it might be more accessible.

P/S: Our 7-seater gas car is quite old, so I'll probably sell it or leave it for anyone who wants to drive or borrow it.

parameter: Country of Origin Image; Price; Technology Features; Competitor Comparison; Dealer Network
content_english: Chinese cars entering the Vietnamese market this time have a long-term strategy: Chery, Geely, and Lynk & Co have already built factories in Vietnam, while BYD is about to launch a strategic PHEV model, the Sealion 6, imported from Thailand. It will be very competitively priced due to lower tariffs compared to importing from China like the other brands. Chinese cars offer high technology, generous features, and decent build quality, but in the Vietnamese market, pricing is crucial (the Xforce and Xpander are examples of how price determines success). I believe Chinese PHEV models will be very viable in Vietnam in the coming period, provided these automakers set up assembly plants and a nationwide network of dealerships and service centers. Only then will competitive prices be possible, allowing them to gradually capture market share from Japanese, Korean, and European brands. Vietnamese consumers will then change their perception of Chinese cars and be ready to drop their prejudices. Japanese, Korean, and European automakers will certainly face major challenges and will have to adjust their strategies. This is also good news for Vietnamese consumers, who will have the opportunity to own better cars in terms of price and quality, without features being cut—for example, a Toyota Fortuner version costing nearly 1.37 billion VND that still requires manual folding mirrors. In short, if Chinese automakers succeed in Vietnam, consumers will be the ones to benefit the most!

content_english: These 7-seater cars are usually service vehicles like the Innova, Expander, Carnival... they cover long distances, and schedules are very unpredictable because they depend on the renter’s needs, so current electric cars can’t meet that demand. Some cars operate under contracts for picking up and dropping off experts from foreign companies on fixed schedules, but that’s not common, so gasoline cars remain the top choice for car owners. At this stage, Vin doesn’t need to invest resources in this vehicle segment. The HAN vehicle fund (pronounced "Han" in Vietnamese) sounds outdated.

category: Brand Perception; Value Perception; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Brand Trust; Value for Money; Energy Consumption
content_english:
The concern about quality is concern based on trust. If you don't trust, don't buy—no need for fake politeness either =))

Frankly, people who buy VF cars do so because they trust the quality (who knows who has better quality, but trust is enough; time will tell). They get price discounts, charging benefits, better charging infrastructure, and they're willing to support a car brand that manufactures in Vietnam. You guys criticize, but the cars you buy—how many units do they sell a year in Vietnam? No one knows. People who praise it buy it, and it sells around a thousand units every month.

Instead of sweating over finding charging stations, instead of spending 200k a day (6 million a month), they take it easy and save 6 million. Got it? And 6 million is nothing—I've seen guys who fill up twice a day, spending 8-9 million a month on gas. Drive for one year, you save an extra 70-100 million.

I drive a VF5, I signed up with GSM to drive extra hours, about 2,000 km a month, earn 7-8 million, free charging for 3.5 years until the end of 2027 (I got a 0.5 year bonus for being an early battery buyer). The car is crappy, ugly, stiff, heavy, decent tech, never spent a single day in the shop.

content_english: About 5 more years when the third-party charging infrastructure fully covers Vietnam, then I'll feel confident buying a BYD car; building out charging stations for EVs is an inevitable necessity. The VF car looks okay on the outside, but the interior is subpar from the seats to the saddle design, the software, and it rattles noisily over speed bumps—if you buy one, you'd be a guinea pig.

parameter: Charging Infrastructure; Maintenance Cost; General Quality; Suitability for Personal Use
content_english: I don't run a ride-hailing service and I'm not comparing any cars, but these guys run a service in a way unlike anyone else. If you're running a service, serving the job is top priority—you need to fully leverage EV infrastructure. BYD may be a good car, but with limited infrastructure, what kind of service is that? Running 80,000 km a year, the service experience is frustrating. In general, for personal use it's okay, but for running a service... This guy seems to heavily praise the choice, but the drawbacks appear to be hidden to justify choosing BYD for ride-hailing.

content_english: Making them but not being able to sell them, it's really hard to force them into taxi use. There's demand for 7-seat taxis, but not much, and you can't charge the same fare as a 4-seat one. Electric cars aren't like gasoline cars—with gas, you can just leave it at a parking lot, plug in to charge the battery, and it's fine. But with an electric car, if you don't let it run to charge the battery, the battery will degrade or die completely. They take the time to launch new models with big media hype, but it still seems pointless... then they force them into taxis again... and there's still VF1, VF2, VF4, VF10 to complete the 10-series lineup.

content_english: I'm talking about EV charging, alright. Go ask the electric car charger seller if they have chargers above 7.5kW for single-phase. I never said it can't be used. You're knowledgeable about electricity, so you probably know what it's like to charge a 130kWh battery at 11kW for over 12 hours straight using standard household wiring. That's likely why they don't sell chargers above 7.5kW for single-phase EV charging. Since household wiring usually uses 6mm² wire or smaller for load handling, it's just not safe over long periods, which is why they don't manufacture them.

content_english: BYD is seriously lacking charging stations, putting it at a disadvantage compared to VinFast. On top of that, it's a Chinese brand—regardless of how that brand performs in China, from a Vietnamese perspective, people here really don't like Chinese products, especially since they just entered Vietnam. As a result, even though the M6 is priced at 756 million, many guys still choose a used 2022-2023 Xpander for commercial use because maintenance is cheap and buying used is also affordable. And if you're looking at the 7-seater family segment, people would probably pick the Innova for peace of mind, rather than a Chinese brand that lacks charging stations. But if it dropped to around 700 million, it might be more accessible.

content_english: I also run a ride-hailing service with my VF6. I once broke out in a cold sweat when the battery hit 0%. The situation was pretty similar to what your friend shared. I was going from Nhơn Trạch to Ho Chi Minh City. 4km before getting on the Cát Lái ferry, the battery dropped to 0%. While on the ferry, I wondered if turning off the car at 0% would recover a bit of charge or if it wouldn’t start again. I decided to keep the car running (not turn it off) all the way to the intersection of Đồng Văn Cống and Ng Thị Định. By then, the battery was at 0%, but the car only allowed me to go 16km/h. The AC turned off by itself, and I continued moving at turtle speed (16km/h) to the charging station, which was another 2.7km away. Believe it or not, the car made it without shutting down. The battery was still at 0% when I reached the charger, and it charged normally. A nerve-wracking and thrilling experience. The VF6’s battery is really impressive—I estimate that at 0%, it could still go another 8km based on how it felt.

content_english: When you have money to buy a car, you'll understand right away, bro. Aside from the car itself, you have to carefully calculate fuel, charging stations, spare parts replacement, and if there's an accident or collision, which repair shop is closest. If you have a car to drive to a province that doesn't have a shop, you'll have to tow it back to Hanoi city, and then you'll feel like a real fool. If you don't plan it out, those miscellaneous costs will eat into your wallet, adding up to the same value as the car you bought. But if you're buying a car just to commute on a fixed route, or to go to cafes and show off to your buddies, then any car will do—why bother thinking about it, bro. If you love it, just buy it.
